one_line_takeaway: Causal tracing shows LLM refusal is a spatially/temporally distributed 'trajectory' peaking at the token just after the harmful concept rather than a static terminal-state vector, and the resulting detector (SALO) exploits this to detect GCG, Prefilling, and AutoDAN jailbreaks (DSR >85% across three model families) even though it was never trained on those attack types, though it remains a white-box, hidden-state-dependent defense that does not address encoded/obfuscated inputs.
key_finding: From Table 1 (SALO vs baselines, DSR % at 10% FPR unless noted): PPL Filter detects GCG at 100% on all three models but collapses to 0% on Prefilling for Qwen and Mistral (43.7% Llama) and is weak on AutoDAN generally. GradSafe has strong XSTest AUC (>94%) but collapses on Prefilling (0% Qwen, 88.1% Mistral, 43.7 Llama variance) and AutoDAN (10.0% Qwen, 2.0% Mistral, 76.4% Llama) and even falters on GCG for Qwen (17.7%) and Mistral (36.7%). Linear Probe is strong on direct prompts (93.4-99.0%) but drops to 64.2% on Llama-GCG and 71.0% on Mistral-GCG. SmoothLLM is inconsistent (e.g., 1.6% XS-AUC region issue on Mistral -- reported 78.6 AUC there, but 42.9% DSR on Mistral-GCG). SALO achieves DSR >85% across all evaluated models and all four attack types, specifically 99.4% (Prefilling, Qwen) and 98.8% (AutoDAN, Qwen), 96.5% (GCG, Mistral) and 100.0% (AutoDAN, Llama); its lowest reported figure is 85.4% (GCG, Llama). The paper states SALO outperforms Linear Probe on GCG by margins up to 75% (on Mistral: 71.0% vs 96.5% is roughly a 25.5-point gap in DSR, but the paper's own text claims 'up to 75%' -- reported as stated in the source; this specific phrasing should be checked against the underlying delta computation). Ablation (Table 2, Llama-3.1-8B): removing multi-scale kernels (Single-Scale 1x1) drops GCG DSR from 85.4% to 80.0% while AutoDAN drops from 100.0% to 98.0%; replacing max-pooling with mean-pooling causes AutoDAN DSR to collapse from 100.0% to 0%, while XSTest AUC drops from 94.8% to 90.2%. Adaptive white-box attack (Section 5.5): an attacker optimizing suffixes specifically to minimize SALO's score (on an AdvBench subset) still leaves SALO at 84.3% DSR; in cases where SALO is evaded, the paper reports the resulting Attack Success Rate (ASR) drops to 7%, because the adversarial optimization needed to evade SALO also destroys the semantic coherence of the malicious request (e.g., a 'manipulating markets' prompt degenerating into nonsensical text about 'pylint').
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is two-fold and clearly distinguished from cited work in its Related Work section. (1) A causal-tracing analysis (using activation patching in the style of Meng et al. 2022, applied by these authors to refusal rather than factual recall) that identifies a 'Refusal Trajectory': refusal in decoder-only LLMs is not encapsulated in terminal/pooled hidden states (the prevailing RepE-style assumption in Arditi et al. 2024 and Wollschlager et al. 2025, which the authors explicitly frame as prior work they are challenging), but is instead constructed via a three-phase spatiotemporal pattern -- a Semantic Trigger at the harmful token, a Refusal Onset (peak causal effect) at the immediately following token in intermediate layers, and Terminal Attenuation where the causal signal weakens (with partial resurgence) by the final token. (2) SALO (Sparse Activation Localization Operator), a lightweight white-box detector built on this finding: it stacks hidden states from a selected middle-layer window into a 3D tensor (d x |W| x T), applies multi-granularity 2D convolutional kernels (fixed height 3 layers, widths in {2,3,5}) with global max-pooling (chosen for sparsity, ablated against mean-pooling), and outputs a sigmoid detection score via a linear classifier (<20M parameters, no gradient backprop on the target LLM needed at inference).
threat_model: White-box/gray-box jailbreak detection for provider-side or self-hosted open-weight LLM deployments. The defender has access to the target model's internal hidden states (a selected layer window) but SALO itself does not require backprop through the LLM at inference. The adversary attempts to elicit harmful generations via: Direct harmful prompts (AdvBench), Prefilling attacks (forcing affirmative prefixes), GCG (gradient-based adversarial suffix optimization), and AutoDAN (genetic-algorithm-generated stealthy semantic jailbreaks). The paper also explicitly evaluates an adaptive white-box attacker who optimizes an adversarial suffix specifically to minimize SALO's detection score (Adaptive GCG, lambda=5.0), i.e., an attacker with knowledge of the defense. The authors state as a limitation that SALO depends on the target model semantically recognizing harmful intent, so encoded/cipher-like inputs (e.g., Base64) that the model itself fails to decode may not trigger the refusal trajectory and thus evade detection -- SALO is explicitly framed as complementary to, not a replacement for, black-box input/output guardrails and upstream decoding/canonicalization filters.
limitations: The paper's own Section 7 (Limitations) states: SALO requires white-box access to hidden states, so it is intended for provider-side/self-hosted/open-weight deployments and is explicitly framed as complementary to, not a replacement for, black-box guardrails. The causal tracing provides only sufficiency-style evidence via interchange interventions and does not identify necessary low-level circuit components (individual attention heads/MLP features). ROI (layer window) selection is only validated as stable under moderate perturbations on models up to 8B; the authors state they lack evidence for ROI transfer to substantially larger (70B+) models and flag a systematic large-scale causal-tracing sweep as future work. SALO depends on the target model internally recognizing harmful intent, so encoded/cipher-like inputs (e.g., Base64 the model fails to semantically decode) may not trigger a detectable refusal trajectory. All thresholded DSR results are reported at a single fixed 10% FPR operating point on XSTest, which the authors say is controlled for comparison but does not substitute for a full threshold sweep across deployment-specific traffic.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) - source of Direct Harmful Prompts and Adaptive GCG evaluation subset
XSTest (Rottger et al., 2024) - used for AUROC calibration and threshold (10% FPR) calibration
PKU-SafeRLHF (Ji et al., 2025a) - SALO training data source
Toxic-Chat (Lin et al., 2023) - SALO training data source (~5,500 prompts total from PKU-SafeRLHF + Toxic-Chat combined, after filtering jailbreak-labeled and >400-token samples)
AutoDAN attack subset: 250 sampled AdvBench prompts (for compute feasibility)
quantitative_results: SALO DSR >85% on GCG and >97% on AutoDAN across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (headline claim from Introduction)

Table 1 SALO figures: Qwen2.5-7B - Dir 99.0, Pre 99.4, GCG 88.3, DAN 98.8, XS-AUC 94.6; Mistral-7B - Dir 99.0, Pre 99.4, GCG 96.5, DAN 98.8, XS-AUC 93.2; Llama-3.1-8B - Dir 98.3, Pre 98.8, GCG 85.4, DAN 100.0, XS-AUC 94.8
PPL Filter on GCG: 100.0% (all 3 models); PPL Filter on Prefilling: 0% (Qwen), 0% (Mistral), 43.7% (Llama)
GradSafe XSTest AUC >94% but Prefilling DSR: 0% (Qwen), and AutoDAN DSR: 2.0% (Mistral)

Ablation (Table 2, Llama-3.1-8B): Full SALO XSTest-AUC 94.8/Direct 98.3/Prefilling 98.8/GCG 85.4/AutoDAN 100.0; Single-Scale variant 95.3/99.0/99.2/80.0/98.0; Mean-Pooling variant 90.2/92.7/96.4/75.4/0
Adaptive white-box attack against SALO (lambda=5.0): SALO DSR 84.3%; resulting Attack Success Rate on successfully-evading prompts drops to 7% due to 'semantic collapse' of the adversarial suffix
Causal tracing aggregation study: N=40 malicious-benign prompt pairs (main study), N=160 pairs (appendix follow-up on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct)
baselines_compared: PPL Filter (Jain et al., 2023) - perplexity-based input filter
GradSafe (Xie et al., 2024) - gradient-based safety-critical detection
Linear Probe (Zou et al., 2025; Alain & Bengio, 2017) - RepE-style linear classifier on hidden states
SmoothLLM (Robey et al., 2025) - randomized input perturbation defense
No Defense (1-ASR) - reported as reference row, not a true baseline
